text: 60 Minutes overtime. What's the worst case scenario that you're worried about is that it is closed for weeks and weeks and weeks here and you start to see the global economy really being impacted because the it is the lifeblood to a certain extent. So the reality is the longer that this goes on, the greater impact it's going to have across industries, across regions. This week on 60 Minutes, we report on the Straight of Hormuz, that narrow waterway on Iran's southern coast. It connects the oil richch countries of the Persian Gulf to the rest of the world. The ships that pass through it carry a fifth of the world's oil and a significant amount of natural gas. Now, with that traffic nearly stopped by the war in Iran, the world is beginning to feel the impact from energy markets to national economies. You can see that there's a lot of movement here, right? >> We spoke with Matt Smith, an oil market analyst for Kepler, which tracks global trade and shipping. He has been studying ship movement in the straight of Hormuz. Smith told us the region moves millions of barrels of oil a day. For most Gulf states, the only way for their oil to reach the global market is through the strait. So, these supplies are stuck because of the conflict. But Saudi Arabia benefits from decades of good planning. >> Saudi Arabia is a relative winner from this situation in that it has a pipeline called the East West pipeline that was built for exactly this reason. It was concerned about the straight of Hormuz being uh this choke point and so that pipe goes across to the Red Sea to Yanu and we're starting to see exports picking up from there instead. So essentially Saudi Arabia is able to reroute its crude to be able to load in the Red Sea whereas the likes of Iraq, Kuwait and Qatar don't have the ability to do that. So they're having to shut in production. Not that Saudi Arabia is to able to completely move all of its barrels and export them from the other side. Uh but it it can do nearly 4 million barrels a day. >> How would you characterize what's happening in the street of Hormuz today? Well, we are seeing a collapse of a keystone assumption in economics and energy markets. And if Hormuz is shut for a month, one of the most important bedrock assumptions and how the economy works, the global economy works and about security will have collapsed. >> Bob McN was an energy adviser to President George W. Bush during the Iraq war, who now advises clients on oil and gas markets. He told us the disruption is already having global impacts, including in Russia. >> This conflict and the oil price increase has done two things to help Putin. One, it's lifted the overall price of crude oil. And secondly, it's eliminated that discount, that penalty price that he had to pay. Now everybody wants his oil. And yeah, Christmas came early for Putin. So Putin may be benefiting temporarily from a budget windfall, but long-term if Iran uh stops being the world's top sponsor as terror and even uh becomes a friend of the United States and Israel and other countries, which I I hope and expect eventually, um that's a bad day for Russia. Very bad day for for Putin. But today uh as things stand in the street of Hormuz, I've seen it said that Russia is the clear winner in this war. >> Putin, no question, is benefiting from much greater revenue right now. Y >> I want to ask you about China. About half of China's imported oil comes through the strait. What does this mean for their economy? >> This is a a real gut punch for their economy. China was getting a sweetheart deal from Iran and uh and depending heavily on flow from Hormuz. The thought that that could be disrupted for a prolonged period is devastating for the Chinese economy. Now, they have stockpiled a lot of oil and so they're pretty well supplied in terms of physical supply, but the economic cost will be quite sharp to them. >> Even so, China is still benefiting from its relationship with Iran. Matt Smith at Kepler told us that Iran has continued to sneak oil out of the straight. He says they've done it by turning off their ship's transponders that show their location, passing through the straight undetected and sending most of the crude oil on board straight to China. That oil is going to China. >> Yeah. >> Let's talk about India. India had been under significant pressure from the US to stop buying Russian oil. What's the case now? India overall is hugely dependent on uh hormuse flows. It does not have extensive strategic stocks like China does like the United States does. India also requires liqufied petroleum gas. It's cooking fuel. Families cook with LPG liqufied petroleum gas that comes from Hormuz and they don't have big stock piles. So for India this is an acute crisis. >> In other words, what's happening in the street of Hormuz impacts the entire world. In the United States, gas prices have already jumped by more than 65 cents a gallon since the war started. And in Asia, which gets most of its oil through the straight, countries like Thailand, Pakistan, and Bangladesh are already dealing with fuel shortages and long lines at gas stations as drivers try to fill up. Jet and diesel fuel prices have also surged, up 25%. And as a result, higher plane tickets and grocery prices are expected to follow. Give it to me straight. What is the worstc case scenario that you are predicting here? >> I'm predicting that this is going to get worse before it gets better. This is going to be a longer and costier conflict than just about everybody expected. >> You have said that a prolonged closure of the street of Hormuz is a guaranteed global recession. Do you still stand by that? >> Look, yes. the the world economy cannot grow without 20% of its energy supply. Period. >> Rarely do we recognize history in the making. But is that what we are witnessing right now? We're sitting in the middle of an unprecedented time. >> Yes, we we are experiencing a just as a factual matter the biggest energy disruption in history. Um, we have to just hope and pray that it doesn't go on for too much longer.
